title: Diplomacy's False Floor: Pakistan Mediates US-Iran Talks While Asia Pays the Price
subhead: Six weeks into the US-Iran ceasefire, Pakistan is scrambling to revive stalled peace talks while the economic damage spreads across Asia and the Middle East. Iran is reviewing Washington's latest proposal, but neither side is bending — and the countries bearing the costs have no seat at the table.

sections.heading: Pakistan's Diplomatic Sprint
sections.body:
Pakistan's army chief visited Tehran on Thursday to push US-Iran peace talks back on track. Six weeks after a fragile ceasefire paused the fighting, negotiations have made little progress. Iran is reviewing the latest US proposal, while President Trump says he's willing to wait 'a couple of days' for Tehran's response — a deadline that keeps shifting.

The mediation effort reveals the structural problem: Pakistan is carrying diplomatic water for negotiations where it has no leverage over the outcome. Islamabad's army chief is shuttling between Washington and Tehran, but the key decisions sit with capitals that are thousands of miles apart in both geography and objectives. Iran's foreign minister stated clearly that Tehran has 'no trust' in the US and will negotiate only if Washington is 'serious' — a signal that the gap remains wide despite six weeks of talking.
sections.whyItMatters: The longer the stalemate persists, the more leverage Iran gains from its de facto control of Hormuz. Every day without resolution normalizes a checkpoint regime that global shipping was never designed to accommodate.

sections.heading: Asia's Economic Wound Deepens
sections.body:
The damage from Hormuz is distributed unevenly, and Asia is absorbing the heaviest costs. Japan imports 95% of its crude from the Middle East, with three-quarters moving through the strait. The Nikkei 225 fell 8% in the conflict's first two weeks. South Korea sources 68% of its crude through Hormuz — roughly 1.7 million barrels per day. The KOSPI dropped over 11%, with circuit breakers triggered on March 4. Both countries are drawing down strategic reserves, but those reserves are finite.

India faces a dual shock: 60% of its oil imports and more than half its natural gas supplies transit Hormuz. Because its LNG contracts are Brent-indexed, the crude spike simultaneously raises oil costs and gas contract prices. Top refining operators have absorbed significant margin compression, while the manufacturing sector faces sustained input cost pressure.

Japan has approached Saudi Arabia for emergency supply increases — a signal that even 254 days of strategic crude reserves cannot wait indefinitely. For South Korea, the threat hits energy-intensive export industries like semiconductor fabrication and petrochemicals that took decades to build and cannot easily pivot.
sections.whyItMatters: The countries paying the highest price for Hormuz's closure have the least influence over its resolution. Japan and South Korea signed a coalition statement supporting safe passage but declined to send warships. The gap between exposure and agency is now the defining feature of this crisis.

sections.heading: The Food Security Chain Reaction
sections.body:
The FAO has warned that time is running out to avoid a serious agrifood crisis in the next 6-12 months. The Persian Gulf accounts for 30-35% of global urea exports and 20-30% of global ammonia exports — the chemical inputs that modern agriculture depends on. Qatar halted LNG production at Ras Laffan after Iranian drone strikes, and India subsequently reduced production at three urea plants. Ethiopia's finance minister called for coordinated international action on fuel and fertilizer pressures.

The chain reaction is straightforward: fertilizer shortages drive up food production costs, which translate into higher food prices for countries already struggling with inflation. The World Food Programme has warned about deepening hunger in Myanmar, while the EU announced €8 million in emergency food aid. These are early-stage responses to a supply disruption that could last months.
sections.whyItMatters: Energy shocks travel through fertilizer into food. The countries least equipped to absorb price spikes — Bangladesh, Pakistan, India, parts of sub-Saharan Africa — are precisely where the fertilizer dependency is highest and the substitution options are fewest.

sections.heading: Jordan's Collision with a War It Didn't Start
sections.body:
Jordan offers a case study in what happens when geography meets a conflict you did not choose. The kingdom has spent 62 days running an active air defense campaign over its own cities. Iran launched at least 262 missiles and drones at 'vital sites' on Jordanian territory — not projectiles transiting toward Israel, but deliberate strikes on military infrastructure including Muwaffaq Salti Air Base. Civil defense teams logged 414 debris incidents. Fragments landed in residential neighborhoods of Irbid, a city of 800,000 people.

The energy shock arrived with equal force. Israel suspended gas exports that had accounted for 85% of Jordan's energy imports and generated 70% of its domestic electricity. The emergency pivot to imported LNG through Aqaba costs an additional $120-140 million per month on a budget already running a $1.5 billion deficit. Tourism — 15% of GDP — collapsed. Petra's cancellations reached 100% in March. April bookings ran 60-65% below projections.

Independent economic modeling places Jordan as the single most exposed non-belligerent importer in the world, with a projected 180-day cumulative GDP loss of -6.35% — worse than Lebanon, Singapore, or Egypt. None of those countries are also intercepting missiles over their cities.
sections.whyItMatters: Jordan did not launch strikes, sign an alliance, or choose the moment. It is paying in missile fragments, empty hotels, emergency fuel imports, and a fiscal gap with no easy solutions. The distance between Washington's decision to strike and Amman's price tag is the structural asymmetry of modern conflict.

sections.heading: China's Calculated Distance
sections.body:
Beijing condemned the war and called for de-escalation, extracted bilateral passage rights for Chinese-flagged vessels through Hormuz, declined to contribute to US-led reopening efforts, and positioned itself through existing investments in Southeast Asian renewable energy infrastructure as the responsible long-term alternative to an America whose foreign policy created the crisis Asia is living through.

China holds approximately 1.2 billion barrels in strategic crude reserves — roughly 108 days of import cover under zero new inflows. Roughly 40% of its oil imports pass through Hormuz, but its coal-heavy energy mix and domestic production capacity provide buffers that Tokyo and Seoul do not have. More importantly, Iran sells approximately 90% of its crude to China — a commercial dependency that has given Chinese vessels practical passage rights even as Western shipping retreats.
sections.whyItMatters: China extracted leverage from a crisis it did not start and has not resolved. By securing passage for its own fleet while declining to help reopen the strait, Beijing demonstrated that the gap between who bears costs and who holds agency is not an accident — it's a feature of how power is distributed in a disrupted system.

sections.heading: Trump Signals Taiwan Call Despite Xi Warning
sections.body:
President Trump said he plans to speak with Taiwan President Lai Ching-te — a move that would mark the first direct conversation between sitting US and Taiwanese presidents since Washington switched diplomatic recognition from Taipei to Beijing in 1979. China's Foreign Ministry urged Washington to handle the Taiwan question with 'utmost prudence' and stop sending 'wrong signals' to Taipei.

Just days after Trump's high-profile Beijing summit with Xi Jinping, where the Chinese president warned that mishandling Taiwan could lead to 'clashes and even conflicts,' Trump is signaling a willingness to upend decades of diplomatic precedent. Taiwan's Foreign Ministry said Lai would be 'happy to talk,' emphasizing the island's commitment to peace and stability. Lai also stressed that purchases of US weapons remain essential for maintaining deterrence across the Taiwan Strait.

Trump approved arms sales to Taiwan while simultaneously praising his relationship with Xi as 'amazing' — a pattern of mixed signals that has raised concerns in Taipei about the consistency of US support. He called a potential Taiwan deal 'a very good negotiating chip.'
sections.whyItMatters: The timing is deliberate. Trump is using Taiwan as leverage in a broader China strategy just days after projecting warmth with Xi in Beijing. The move tests whether Beijing's warnings carry consequences, and whether the US-Taiwan relationship can survive being treated as a bargaining chip in Washington's negotiations with the mainland.

bottomLine: Six weeks into the ceasefire, the US-Iran conflict has settled into a diplomatic stalemate that extracts costs from those least able to influence its outcome. Pakistan is mediating but has no leverage. Iran is reviewing proposals but signals no urgency. Trump says he can wait. Meanwhile, Japan and South Korea burn through strategic reserves, Jordan intercepts missiles over cities it did not target, India's fertilizer supply chain faces disruption, and China secures passage rights for its own fleet while declining to help others. The FAO warns of a food crisis in 6-12 months. Trump's signal of a direct Taiwan call tests Beijing's tolerance just days after a summit designed to project stability. The gap between who pays and who decides has become the central fact of this war — and it is widening, not closing.
